mnemonics.title: OS SERVICES MEMORY AID
mnemonics.content: PEMIFAD (Program Execution, Memory Management, Information Maintenance, File Management, Accounting, Device Management). ADD (Authentication, Deadlock handling, Error Detection) and PROTECT (Protection) and COMM (Communication) and NETWORK.

mnemonics.title: USER INTERFACE TYPES
mnemonics.content: CLI (Command Line Interface) - TEXT BASED, GUI (Graphical User Interface) - VISUAL BASED, TUI (Touch-based User Interface) - GESTURE BASED. REMEMBER: C-G-T - ALL INTERFACES.

mnemonics.title: PROCESS MANAGEMENT FUNCTIONS
mnemonics.content: CREATE, SCHEDULE, TERMINATE, SYNCHRONIZE - CST. PROCESS LIFECYCLE: NEW -> READY -> RUNNING -> WAITING -> TERMINATED.

acronyms.usage: Text-based interface where users type commands to interact with the OS. Examples: Bash, PowerShell, Terminal.
Visual interface using windows, icons, menus, and pointers. Examples: Windows, macOS, GNOME.
Data structure maintained by OS kernel that contains information about a process (state, PC, registers, memory map, open files).
Mechanisms for processes to communicate and synchronize, including pipes, message queues, shared memory, and sockets.
Memory technology that detects and corrects data corruption, used by Error Detection service.
Security mechanism that specifies which users or processes are allowed to access particular resources.
Set of functions and procedures that allow applications to use OS services programmatically.

visualTips.visualization: Draw a pyramid: BOTTOM (Hardware) -> MIDDLE (OS Services layer) -> TOP (User Applications). Services sit between hardware and user programs.
Use a circular diagram: NEW -> READY (queue) -> RUNNING (CPU) -> WAITING (I/O) -> READY -> TERMINATED. Arrows show state transitions.
Draw memory as a rectangle divided into: OS kernel area, user program area, free space. Show paging using grid boxes (pages) and frames.
Show layered approach: Application -> OS -> Device Driver -> Hardware Device. Each layer abstracts the one below it.

analogies.analogy: OS is like a hotel manager. It allocates rooms (memory) to guests (processes), handles housekeeping (device management), maintains guest records (accounting), provides reception services (user interface), and ensures security (protection).
Process management is like a factory supervisor. It creates work orders (processes), schedules work on machines (CPU), coordinates workers (threads), and completes tasks (execution).
Memory management is like a librarian. It keeps track of which books (data) are checked out (allocated), manages the shelf space (memory), and returns books to shelves when done (deallocation).
File management is like a filing cabinet system. It organizes files in folders (directories), maintains indexes (file allocation tables), and controls access to sensitive documents (permissions).
